context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Actors: State

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the role of the State in development planning? Is the 'Third World State' expected to behave differently from the 'First World' State in its relation to industry, private sector, financial markets and social programs? What is the impact of globalization on the State? What distinguishes a State from other actors in planning?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m really stuck on this reflection about the role of the State in development planning. The prompt has like four big questions in one, and they all blur together for me. Could you help me figure out what it’s actually asking?
exchanges.tutor: That's a fair observation — it is a dense prompt! Let's slow down. If you had to pick just ONE of those four questions as the most central one — the one the others seem to orbit around — which would it be, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the very beginning and feeling overwhelmed by the multi-part prompt. My goal is to help them break it down themselves using guided discovery rather than explaining it to them. I'll ask a simple orienting question that gets them to actively parse the prompt rather than doing it for them. Minimal scaffolding needed at this stage.

exchanges.student: Maybe the main one is just “what is the role of the State in development planning,” but then I don’t really know what counts as “role” here. I’m also not sure if I’m supposed to define the State in general or compare rich and poor countries right away.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct identifying that central question. Here's a simpler way in: the prompt itself asks what distinguishes the State from *other* actors in planning. If you can answer that, 'role' might become clearer. So — what do you think makes the State different from, say, an NGO or a private company operating in the same country?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the central question, which is good progress. Now they're wrestling with two sub-questions: what 'role' means, and how to structure their response. Rather than explaining either, I'll nudge them to think about what distinguishes the State from other actors first — which is actually one of the prompt's own questions and a natural entry point. This keeps the discovery process going with minimal scaffolding.

exchanges.student: I guess the State has actual authority, but that still feels too vague to me. Like NGOs and companies affect development too, so I’m not seeing how to turn “the State has authority” into a full reflection without just saying obvious stuff.
exchanges.tutor: You're closer than you think. But let's sharpen it: an NGO can *influence* development, a company can *invest* in it — but what can the State do that neither of those actors can? Think about laws, taxes, borders, force. What's the word for that kind of power?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is onto something real with 'authority' but is underselling it and worried about depth. I should push them to be more precise about what kind of authority — the course syllabus specifically emphasizes the state as 'rule-maker' and the link between planning, power and legitimacy. I won't give that framing directly, but I can ask a question that nudges them toward it.

exchanges.student: Is it sovereignty? I kind of know that word, but I’m fuzzy on how to use it here, and then I start mixing it up with legitimacy and governance and everything turns into abstract buzzwords for me.
exchanges.tutor: Sovereignty is a good word here. Let's ground it so it doesn't stay abstract. Can you think of a real example — any country, any situation — where the State used its unique authority to shape development in a way no other actor could have?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has landed on sovereignty, which is a strong and relevant concept. They're now worried about getting lost in abstract terminology. Rather than defining these terms for them, I should ask them to ground 'sovereignty' in a concrete example — this will help them see the difference between sovereignty, legitimacy, and governance naturally, without me just listing definitions. Keep it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: I’m blanking, honestly. My brain jumps to “the government built infrastructure” or “the government regulated markets,” but that feels way too generic, and I can’t tell what kind of example would actually fit this class.
exchanges.tutor: Actually, market regulation is a perfectly reasonable example — don't dismiss it. Now make it specific: can you think of a non-Western country where the government *deliberately* shaped its industrial or economic development through regulation or investment? Even something you've heard of vaguely in the news or in your readings is fine.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is actually giving decent examples but dismissing them as too generic. I should validate that infrastructure and market regulation are actually fine starting points, and push them to think more specifically about a developing country context — which is what the course is about. I'll ask a simple follow-up that connects their example to the course's focus on non-Western states.

exchanges.student: Maybe South Korea? But then I get nervous because I don’t know if that counts as “Third World” in the way the prompt means, and I also don’t know if I’m supposed to talk about industrial policy, welfare, globalization, and state legitimacy all at once.
exchanges.tutor: South Korea is actually a great example for this prompt — hold onto it. And here's something worth thinking about: the prompt asks if the 'Third World State' is *expected* to behave differently from the 'First World' State. Does South Korea's development story suggest anything about that expectation — or complicate it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: South Korea is an excellent example — it's a classic case study in developmental state theory, highly relevant to this course. The student is now second-guessing themselves and also feeling re-overwhelmed by the prompt's scope. I should affirm the example briefly and redirect them to the 'Third World State' question in the prompt, which South Korea actually helps illuminate. I should also gently remind them that a reflection piece doesn't require exhaustive coverage of every sub-question.

exchanges.student: Yeah, I think that’s where I get lost — “expected” feels like it’s asking about some theory or stereotype, not just what happened. So then I start thinking maybe I’m supposed to explain the developmental state, neoliberalism, globalization, welfare, all of it, and it suddenly feels huge again.
exchanges.tutor: Your instinct about 'expected' is sharp — it really is pointing to an external pressure or prescription, not just description. But here's a reality check: this is 700-800 words. You cannot cover everything, and you're not supposed to. What ONE tension or argument do you think you could build a focused reflection around, using South Korea as your anchor?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is spiraling again into feeling overwhelmed by scope. This is the third time they're expressing anxiety about covering everything. I should be a bit direct here: a 700-800 word reflection doesn't require exhaustive coverage. I should also validate their instinct about 'expected' pointing to theory/stereotype — that's actually a sharp observation worth building on. I'll ask them to focus on just that word 'expected' to help them see the reflection has a clear argument to make, not a textbook to reproduce.

exchanges.student: Maybe that the State is supposed to create development, but it’s also pressured by globalization to step back? I’m not totally sure if that’s too simplistic, and I still can’t picture how that becomes a whole reflection instead of a couple vague paragraphs.
exchanges.tutor: That tension is a solid foundation — it's not simplistic at all. Now think about this: to make that argument convincing in your reflection, what would you need to show the reader? What's the minimum evidence or reasoning that makes the tension real rather than just asserted?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually landed on a solid central tension for their reflection — state-led development vs. pressure to retreat under globalization. This is genuinely good and I should affirm it without over-praising. They're now worried about structure/length, which is a different concern. Rather than explaining how to structure a reflection, I should ask them what they'd need to show to make that tension convincing — this gets them thinking about evidence and argument naturally. We're also at a point where I should push toward actual writing soon.

exchanges.student: I think I’d need to show that the State has some unique power in planning, then show a case where it used that power strongly, and then show how globalization limited or changed that. But I still feel like I’m one step away from understanding it, like I can say the pieces but not how they fit together.
exchanges.tutor: You just outlined your reflection — those three pieces *are* the structure. The reason it still feels disconnected is probably because you haven't written it yet. Talking about it and writing it are different. Try drafting just your opening paragraph based on what you told me, and bring it back. That's where the pieces will start clicking.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now articulated a clear three-part structure for their reflection on their own. This is excellent progress — they've essentially outlined their paper. The feeling of pieces not fitting is likely because they haven't tried writing yet. We've had multiple exchanges building up to this point, and this is a natural moment to push them toward a first writing attempt rather than continuing to talk about it. I should acknowledge their progress clearly and redirect to writing.

exchanges.student: I still don’t really know what an opening paragraph for this is supposed to sound like. Could you just explain the whole prompt more directly, because I feel like I’m piecing together fragments and missing the big picture?
exchanges.tutor: I hear the frustration, but I want to be honest with you: you already understand the prompt. In this conversation alone you identified the central question, came up with a relevant example, named a real tension, and outlined a three-part structure. That's the big picture. I won't explain it further — that's your job as the writer. Open a document and write one sentence that states your argument. Just one.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to essentially explain the whole prompt for them, which would cross the line into giving the answer directly. This is also likely a moment of frustration rather than genuine confusion — they've actually demonstrated strong understanding throughout this conversation. I should be honest and direct about that, remind them of my role, and push them firmly toward writing rather than more discussion.
